stories.title: Secrecy Is Killing Data Center Deals
stories.excerpt: The data center industry does not have a communication problem. It has a transparency problem. Across the country, projects are being delayed, contested and, in some cases, stopped before construction begins. The common thread is not always power or water. It is process. Communities are finding out too late, with too little information, about projects that will reshape their local infrastructure. That is not a public relations issue. It is a development failure. The use of nondisclosure agreements, redacted filings and accelerated approvals has become common in many jurisdictions. These tools are often justified as necessary to protect proprietary information or to maintain competitive positioning. Suhail Y. Tayeb. PHOTO: Courtesy NYU In some cases, they are justified. But, when projects rely too heavily on secrecy, they create the very opposition they are trying to avoid. When residents discover that key decisions were made behind closed doors, trust collapses. When basic information about water use, energy demand or site impact is withheld, communities assume the worst. Once that perception takes hold, the project is no longer being evaluated on its merits. It is being rejected on principle. The industry often treats transparency as a concession — something to offer once a project is already underway or facing resistance. That approach is backward. Transparency is not a moral position. It is a development strategy. Projects that disclose early, clearly and consistently tend to move faster. They face fewer surprises in public hearings, fewer legal challenges and less political resistance. They give communities time to understand, question and adjust before positions harden. Projects that do not take this approach tend to follow a predictable path. Late disclosure, rapid opposition, prolonged delays and, in some cases, cancellation. This pattern is now visible across multiple states. And it is accelerating. What was once a localized reaction has evolved into a more
bullets: Data center projects delayed/stopped by community opposition from lack of transparency.
NDAs, redacted filings, accelerated approvals hide water, energy, infrastructure impacts.
Trust collapse when residents learn decisions made behind closed doors.
stories.summary: Data center development is increasingly stalled not by power or water constraints, but by secrecy in the permitting process. Use of nondisclosure agreements, redacted filings, and opaque approvals justify themselves as competitive protection but actually trigger community opposition and project rejection. Once residents discover key decisions were made without public input, projects lose credibility regardless of merits.
stories.impact: Multifamily developers in markets attracting data center clusters (Austin, Phoenix, Northern Virginia, DFW) should expect similar transparency pressures and community backlash on large-scale projects. Early, proactive stakeholder engagement and clear environmental/infrastructure disclosures reduce entitlement risk and timeline extension.

stories.title: Stephen Wendell of Mountain Shore Properties: 5 Questions
stories.excerpt: You can trace the history of Mountain Shore Properties back to the 1980s, when the firm had no name and was run by Stephen Wendell’s father, Charlie Wendell. In 1983, the older Wendell founded the company by opening his first Holiday Inn hotel in Oak Hill, W.Va., not far from where he was born and was raising a family. When Stephen Wendell joined the company in 2012 — following law school and a stint as a junior legal counsel with the Philadelphia Eagles — he knew it was time to grow the business, feeling that there was so much more the firm could do. Now, with the younger Wendell at the helm as CEO, Mountain Shore is a national real estate development company with over $400 million in project value, focusing on a mix of hospitality, residential and office properties. Its portfolio spans more than 25 cities across 11 states. And it’s also about to open its 30th hotel, a boutique inn in the Wendell family’s hometown in West Virginia. Commercial Observer caught up with Stephen Wendell last month to discuss the founding of Mountain Shore, the firm’s portfolio, and the lessons each new project imparts. This conversation has been edited for clarity and length. Commercial Observer: What is the history of Mountain Shore Properties? Stephen Wendell: Mountain Shore Properties essentially emanated from the real estate development company that my dad started in the early `80s. There was no name for the company at that point in time, but he started doing real estate development in the `80s — hotels specifically. He opened his first hotel the month I was born, so almost 43 years ago, in our small town in Fayetteville, W.Va. There’s now this full-circle moment, because we’re going to open a small boutique hotel in Fayetteville in August. When I got out of law school, I worked in Philadelphia for a year for the Eagles, and was doing real estate on the side, and then went to work for a law firm. After a while, I told my dad I wanted to work with him, and that I had ideas on how to
bullets: Mountain Shore Properties: $400M portfolio, 30 hotels, 25 cities, 11 states.
CEO Stephen Wendell (since 2012) diversifying beyond hospitality into residential, office.
No specific market signals or demand drivers disclosed in profile.
stories.summary: Mountain Shore Properties is a regional developer founded in 1983, now run by second-generation CEO Stephen Wendell, with $400M in assets across hospitality, residential, and office in 25 cities. The firm is opening its 30th hotel and has expanded into residential development. No quantifiable market trends, financings, or capacity changes are disclosed.
stories.impact: Minimal direct impact. Profile is biographical rather than market-moving; lacks insight into where the firm is acquiring, what rents/yields drive underwriting, or whether residential focus signals shift toward higher-growth markets. No signal for broader multifamily supply or demand.

stories.title: Land Use Intelligence Is Increasingly a Must-Have in Deal-Making
stories.excerpt: For decades, commercial real estate underwriting has revolved around one central question: Do the numbers work? Investors scrutinize rent rolls, operating expenses, cap rates, financing assumptions and projected returns in pursuit of the answer. Financial models have become increasingly sophisticated, enabling market participants to evaluate opportunities faster and with greater precision than ever before. Yet some of the most costly mistakes in commercial real estate aren’t caused by faulty math, but rather by assumptions. A site that appears attractive on paper may have property characteristics or local considerations that materially impact its investment potential. A redevelopment opportunity may look compelling until additional factors affect project economics, timelines or execution risk. In each case, the pro forma may have been accurate, but the underlying assumptions were not. The industry’s challenge has never been a lack of financial analysis. It’s that critical information influencing those analyses has historically lived outside the underwriting process. Kyle Cowherd. Photo: Courtesy Crexi. Commercial real estate professionals have long understood the importance of zoning, land use regulations and entitlement considerations. The problem has been gaining this critical access. Unlike financial information, which has become increasingly standardized and centralized, zoning data remains fragmented across thousands of municipal jurisdictions. Every city, county and municipality maintains its own terminology, documentation standards and reporting systems. What should be a straightforward diligence process often requires navigating multiple government websites, reviewing lengthy zoning codes and manually interpreting permitted uses. As a result, zoning has traditionally been treated as a downstream diligence exercise rather than an underwriting input. That approach made sense when accessing land use information required significant time and ef
bullets: Land use intelligence now critical to CRE underwriting beyond financial models.
Site assumptions (zoning, local factors, property characteristics) drive execution risk.
Pro formas accurate but underlying assumptions flawed; non-financial data historically outside models.
stories.summary: Commercial real estate has relied on sophisticated financial underwriting, but critical failures arise from faulty non-financial assumptions about site characteristics, zoning, and local conditions. Land use intelligence—historically segregated from financial analysis—now must inform underwriting to avoid costly redevelopment delays and execution risk.
stories.impact: Multifamily developers must embed zoning, entitlement timeline, infrastructure adequacy, and community/regulatory trends into pro forma assumptions earlier; relying solely on rent/cap rate models exposes projects to timeline creep and cost overruns. This is especially acute in supply-constrained, high-regulation metros (LA, NYC, San Francisco, Denver).

stories.title: Inland Real Estate Group Offloads Lakewood Apartment Community for $71.5M
stories.excerpt: Inland Real Estate Group sold Westlink at Oak Station at 1665 Pierson St. in Lakewood for $71.5 million. Inland bought the property in 2016 for $63 million. Amenities at the community include a pool and hot tub, fitness and business centers, and a clubhouse. The acquisition was facilitated by CBRE’s Denver Multifamily team, which was led by Shane Ozment and Terrance Hunt. Debt financing was arranged by CBRE’s Debt and Structured Finance group led by Ryan Greer and Troy Tegeler. For nearly six decades, Inland’s dozen member companies have supported investment, development, and finance products through an end-to-end suite of commercial real estate services spanning acquisitions, asset management, property management, and operations. The post Inland Real Estate Group Offloads Lakewood Apartment Community for $71.5M appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Inland Real Estate sold Westlink at Oak Station (Lakewood) for $71.5M in 2024.
Bought property 2016 for $63M; realized 13.5% gain over 8 years.
CBRE's Denver team facilitated sale; debt arranged by CBRE.
stories.summary: Inland Real Estate exited the Westlink at Oak Station apartment community in Lakewood, Colo., for $71.5M, eight years after acquiring it for $63M. The sale was brokered and financed by CBRE, suggesting the market is absorbing secondary-market multifamily at moderate appreciation. No cap rate or buyer profile disclosed.
stories.impact: Minimal signal. Modest 1.6% annualized return over 8 years suggests slower Denver rent growth than peers or value stagnation; likely a portfolio-trimming exit. No indication of broader demand shift or capital flows into/out of the Denver metro. Market fundamentals not disclosed.

stories.title: Blake Investment wins bid for $8B Tropicana Field redevelopment
stories.excerpt: A team led by Blake Investment Partners won a bid to redevelop St. Petersburg’s Historic Gas Plant District, home to Tropicana Field and one of the most sought-after development opportunities in the Southeastern United States. St. Petersburg Mayor Ken Welch selected Blake Investment Partners, led by St. Petersburg native Thompson Blake, to redevelop the 86-acre site, the Tampa Bay Times reported. Blake’s team includes the Related Group and hedge fund Elliott Investment Management. The firm beat out the competing finalist with “The Burg Bid,” a proposal to purchase 58 acres for $275 million and pour $8.1 billion into the project. […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Blake Investment + Related + Elliott selected to redevelop 86-acre Tropicana Field site, St. Petersburg.
$8.1B project, 3,600 affordable units (buy/rent), $275M land purchase.
2-decade build, first building: Woodson African American Museum.
stories.summary: Blake Investment Partners (led by St. Petersburg native Thompson Blake), partnering with Related Group and hedge fund Elliott Investment Management, won a bid to redevelop the Historic Gas Plant District in St. Petersburg, Fla. The $8.1B, 86-acre project includes demolition of Tropicana Field and development of 3,600 income-restricted units, parks, cultural anchors, and museum. Land purchase is $275M; two-decade timeline expected.
stories.impact: Major demand signal for South Florida multifamily. A $8.1B, mixed-use redevelopment with 3,600 units signals strong investor confidence and likely municipal incentives (tax abatement, density relief) for the St. Petersburg market. The inclusion of affordable housing and cultural anchors suggests below-market rents on portion of units; unsubsidized market-rate rents will be critical to feasibility. This is third developer selected in 6 years—prior delays signal entitlement/financing friction. Monitor land cost basis, soft costs, and timeline slippage as proxy for broader South Florida development risk.

stories.title: Stevenson affiliate cuts unit count at proposed Glendale multifamily project
stories.excerpt: A developer is downsizing its proposed multifamily development in Glendale more than two years after the plans first surfaced.  A new application filed with the City of Glendale outlines new plans from 550 Riverdale LLC, the owner of the development site at 2748 San Fernando Road in the city’s Pacific-Edison neighborhood, Urbanize Los Angeles reported. The LLC is linked in state business records to David Stevenson, president of Glendale-based Stevenson Real Estate Services, and the real estate firm’s Glendale address. In 2024, the Robert and Stefanie Stevenson Family Trust — the property owner at the time — filed an application […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Stevenson affiliate cuts Glendale project from 249 units to 229 units.
Glendale needs 13,425 units by 2029; project pursues 7-story density bonus.
27 affordable units offered in exchange for entitlement relief.
stories.summary: A Stevenson Real Estate Services affiliate reduced its proposed multifamily project at 2748 San Fernando Road in Glendale from 249 to 229 units, seeking a seven-story entitlement (above the six-story zoning cap) via density bonus. The revised proposal dedicates 27 units (11.8%) as affordable. Glendale must deliver 13,425 new units by 2029 to meet state RHNA targets.
stories.impact: Demand signal for LA-area multifamily. Glendale has only ~5 years to deliver 13,425 units (2,685 units/year); current pipeline insufficient. A unit count reduction mid-entitlement suggests either financing tightness, rent growth slowing, or density bonus incentives (affordability requirement) making project marginal. However, city RHNA shortfall is acute—expect aggressive entitlement approvals and possible land cost deflation as developers compete for approvals. Monitor affordable housing requirements; if >15% hard-forced, market-rate economics weaken.

stories.title: LCOR lands $193M construction loan for Edgewater tower amid flurry of apartment projects
stories.excerpt: LCOR scored a $192.5 million construction loan for a 544-unit luxury apartment tower in Edgewater, as developers are excitedly pursuing multifamily projects across South Florida, despite a lingering oversupply.  New York-based LCOR started construction of a 39-story building at 1775 Biscayne Boulevard in Miami, according to the developer’s news release. The 1.1-acre site is a block from waterfront and Margaret Pace Park. The construction loan amounts to $354,000 per unit. Designed by ODP Architect, with interiors by Asprea Studio, the tower will include more than 40,000 square feet of amenities, over 10,000 square feet of ground-floor retail and a 448-space […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: LCOR secures $192.5M construction loan for 544-unit luxury tower, Edgewater, Miami.
South Florida delivered 18,600 units in 2024, outpacing 2024 leasing by ~20%.
Construction costs $354K/unit; oversupply driving rents down despite strong financing.
stories.summary: LCOR landed a $192.5M construction loan from Natixis for a 544-unit luxury apartment tower at 1775 Biscayne Boulevard in Edgewater, Miami, with expected completion Q4 2028. The loan works out to $354K per unit. Despite lingering oversupply in South Florida (2024 saw 18,600 unit deliveries vs. demand, pushing rents lower), developers continue pursuing multifamily. LCOR paid $49M for the site in 2022.
stories.impact: South Florida multifamily is overbuilt with negative fundamental momentum. 18,600 units delivered in 2024 outpaced absorption by 20%, yet lenders are still funding new supply at $354K/unit—suggesting either faith in rent recovery, refinance/exit timing, or lender risk appetite not yet corrected. Developers pursuing projects in this environment are likely chasing density bonuses, waterfront premiums, or luxury positioning to justify rents. Risk: deliveries in 2028 will hit a market still absorbing 2024–2025 excess. Monitor absorption rates and rent trends in 2025–2026; if negative, construction loan deferrals/breaches likely in 2027–2028.

stories.title: David Martin in talks to join Related, BH on Hollywood Beach Resort redevelopment
stories.excerpt: Developer David Martin is in talks to join the Related Group and BH Group on their planned redevelopment of the Hollywood Beach Resort, sources told The Real Deal.  The Pérez family’s Coconut Grove-based Related and Isaac and Liat Toledano’s Aventura-based BH Group are under contract to acquire the 4.5-acre property at 101 North Ocean Drive, as previously reported by TRD. The Chetrit Group is selling the century-old resort. A source said the property is under contract for $100 million.  Martin, CEO of Terra Group declined to comment through a spokesperson. In a statement, BH named Terra and Related as its […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: David Martin (Terra Group CEO) joining Related + BH on Hollywood Beach Resort redevelopment.
Related + BH under contract for 4.5-acre oceanfront property at $100M.
Project redeveloping 398-unit resort; funding, unit count, timeline not disclosed.
stories.summary: Terra Group CEO David Martin is in talks to join Related Group and BH Group on the redevelopment of the 4.5-acre Hollywood Beach Resort (101 North Ocean Drive). Related and BH are under contract to acquire the century-old, 398-unit resort from the Chetrit Group for ~$100M. The partnership plans redevelopment; exact unit count and scope not yet disclosed. Related and BH have over 20 projects together across South Florida.
stories.impact: South Florida waterfront supply signal. Hollywood Beach Resort redevelopment adds oceanfront multifamily supply in a prime South Beach submarket; $100M land basis + $398-unit existing portfolio suggests developers expect <$250K/unit all-in cost or significant unit count increase via entitlements. Chetrit's financial distress (lender foreclosure filings in 2024) signals distressed exit; successor owner (Related/BH/Terra) likely to stabilize and optimize. Monitor entitlement approvals and timeline; waterfront South Florida projects often face environmental/parking/design review delays.

stories.title: Whirlybird dreams: Ken Griffin wants helipad for Miami Beach megayacht marina
stories.excerpt: Billionaire Citadel founder Ken Griffin has already conquered the land and the sea in Miami Beach. His next target? The air.  City commissioners approved Griffin’s massive private megayacht marina at 120 MacArthur Causeway a few months ago, but now Griffin wants to chopper in. He submitted a request for a private, waterfront helipad at his sprawling Terminal Island development, the Miami Herald reported. The proposal, sponsored by Miami Beach Commissioner Joseph Magazine, is navigating the city’s Land Use and Sustainability Committee and the Planning Board. To clear the runway for Griffin, officials would need to amend Miami Beach’s land development […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Ken Griffin seeking private helipad at Terminal Island megayacht marina, Miami Beach.
Miami commissioners approving waterfront industrial/luxury development; zoning amendment required.
Non-residential development; no direct multifamily impact.
stories.summary: Billionaire Citadel founder Ken Griffin has requested approval for a private helipad at his planned megayacht marina at Terminal Island (120 MacArthur Causeway) in Miami Beach. The 3.5-acre development, approved for eight megayacht slips, requires amendment to land-use regulations to permit helipads as accessory use in industrial zones. Griffin acquired the site from Related Group and is adjacent to Star Island residential holdings.
stories.impact: Minimal multifamily relevance. This is ultra-luxury amenity/waterfront industrial zoning. Indirectly: Miami Beach's willingness to amend zoning for high-net-worth individuals' projects may signal flexibility in density/use entitlements more broadly, but this is speculative. No impact on apartment demand, supply, or capital flows.

stories.title: Houston’s office glut is fueling a demolition trend for mostly vacant properties
stories.excerpt: As the Houston office market struggles to recover at the same rate as other similar cities, sometimes just tearing the buildings down is better for everyone involved.  Record low office vacancies and a need for private demolition has led to more demand than capital available for demolition work, according to Bisnow. Houston had an office vacancy rate of 26.8 percent, according to the outlet, a staggering figure attributable to old or obsolete office buildings and the pandemic ripple effect on employee hybrid schedules.  Vacant or near-vacant buildings all over the city can’t be demolished due to debt and lenders that […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Houston office vacancy 26.8%; demand for demolition exceeds capital available.
Grant Mackay Demolition Co. reports demolition demand outstrips capability.
Galleria submarket showed 200K SF positive absorption in 2025; broader Houston negative.
stories.summary: Houston's office market is experiencing record-high vacancy (26.8%), driving demolition of obsolete or vacant buildings. Demolition demand exceeds available capital and contractor capacity, according to Grant Mackay Demolition. While specific submarkets like Galleria showed 200K SF positive absorption in 2025, Houston's annual office absorption remains negative and lags recovery rates in Dallas and other Texas cities.
stories.impact: Indirect multifamily supply opportunity in Houston. Demolished office buildings create redevelopment sites; absent office-to-multifamily conversion economics (common in LA, NYC), demolished sites are often land-banked or rezoned for owner-occupancy. If Houston's office glut persists, look for opportunistic land acquisition by multifamily developers targeting 2026–2027 entitlements. However, current negative absorption and rent pressure suggest Houston apartment market is not tight enough to absorb new supply; delayed demolition-to-development conversions likely.

stories.title: Inflationary Concerns Send School Shoppers Back to Discount Stores: Report
stories.excerpt: Parents’ back-to-school budgets are up nearly 12 percent this year, according to JLL’s 2026 Back-to-School Survey, and that’s good news for discount stores. The report, released Thursday, saw the dollar store sector enter the survey’s top 10 list of parents’ most-cited shopping destinations. The sector’s rising star comes as Americans continue to tighten belts in the face of inflation. The brokerage’s online survey collected responses from 1,022 U.S. parents with children between the ages of 5 and 18. Its release coincides with the July back-to-school shopping surge, when 27 percent of surveyed parents plan to check off their children’s classroom wish lists. Over 90 percent of parents plan to shop at physical stores this year, according to the survey, and dollar stores and mass merchants dominated their spending plans. The popularity of cheaper retailers such as Dollar Tree and Walmart are no surprise –– the report found school shopping budgets rising 11.7 percent from last year, up to an average of $489 per child. The uptick runs well ahead of the current U.S. inflation rate of around 4 percent. “Families are seeking retailers that respect their time and budgets, which explains why 68.7 percent cite saving money, and 35.1 percent prioritize finding the full school list in one place,” James Cook, JLL’s Americas director of retail research, said in a statement. The largest budget hike was found among middle-income families, who raised their spending expectations roughly 20 percent this year to $495 per child. The number of discretionary items on parents’ shopping lists fell in kind for the second consecutive year, meaning fewer fidget spinners and novelty pens will be landing in shopping carts. Consumers are shopping more strategically, the report said, with stores like Dollar General and Dollar Tree gaining ground for their discounts, and mass retailers like Walmart taking the top spot for their one-stop-shop convenience. Walmart popularity r
bullets: JLL 2026 Back-to-School Survey: parent budgets up 11.7% to avg $489/child.
Dollar store sector entered survey's top 10 shopping destinations.
Over 90% of parents plan physical store shopping despite inflation concerns.
stories.summary: JLL's 2026 Back-to-School Survey found parent shopping budgets increased 11.7% year-over-year to an average of $489 per child, despite inflation. Dollar stores entered the survey's top 10 list of shopping destinations. Over 90% of surveyed parents plan to shop at physical retail locations, with mass merchants and discount retailers dominating spending.
stories.impact: No material impact on multifamily development. Retail spending trends (shift to discount stores) are not correlated with housing demand, supply, or capital. This is a consumer sentiment/retail health indicator, not a labor market, migration, or housing demand signal relevant to apartment developers.

stories.title: CBRE Opens Hospitality-Inspired Office in Columbus
stories.excerpt: CBRE announced the opening of its new 19,500-square-foot office on the fifth floor at 10 W. Nationwide Blvd., a trophy office building within the Arena District owned and developed by Nationwide Realty Investors in downtown Columbus, Ohio. The firm has relocated from 200 Civic Center Drive. The new office is part of CBRE’s Workplace360 program, which showcases technology and a wide variety of collaborative spaces designed to support hybrid working. “As one of Columbus’s top trophy office buildings, 10 W. Nationwide Blvd. is an ideal home, and CBRE is proud to be its first tenant,” said CBRE’s Michael Copella, Executive Managing Director and Market Leader, Central Ohio. “The office design reflects the spirit of Columbus and its distinctive culture while highlighting the scope of CBRE’s capabilities.” New technology is installed throughout the office to support remote and in-person collaboration between employees. The office’s design reflects the unique character of Columbus, drawing inspiration from bespoke hotels and hospitality-focused experiences. The post CBRE Opens Hospitality-Inspired Office in Columbus appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: CBRE relocates Columbus office (19.5K SF) to 10 W. Nationwide Blvd., Arena District.
New office uses Workplace360 hospitality-inspired design for hybrid work.
Building owned/developed by Nationwide Realty Investors; first major tenant announced.
stories.summary: CBRE opened a 19,500-square-foot office in downtown Columbus at 10 W. Nationwide Blvd., an Arena District trophy office building owned by Nationwide Realty Investors. The space is designed under CBRE's Workplace360 program, featuring hospitality-inspired aesthetics and technology for hybrid work collaboration. This is the building's first announced tenant.
stories.impact: Minimal multifamily relevance. This is office leasing/workplace design, not a demand signal for apartments. Indirectly: CBRE's relocation to a premium downtown building suggests confidence in Columbus's Central Business District and office-using tenants, but Columbus's multifamily market is not supply-constrained; no cap rate or rent pressure signal.

stories.title: JLL Arranges Sale of Lincoln Park Retail Center to Curbline Properties
stories.excerpt: JLL has secured the sale of Clybourn Center, a 33,140-square-foot unanchored strip center strategically located in Chicago’s Lincoln Park neighborhood. JLL’s Capital Market Investment and Sales Advisory team, representing the seller, was led by Managing Director Michael Nieder and Director Brian Page. Leasing efforts at the center have been spearheaded by Steve Schwartz and his team. The buyer was Curbline Properties. Clybourn Center is strategically positioned at 2029-2073 N. Clybourn Ave., across from Foundry Park, a transformational $1 billion-plus mixed-use redevelopment project. When complete, Foundry Park will deliver approximately 3,700 residential units, 350,000 square feet of office space, 420,000 square feet of retail space, 250,000 square feet of hospitality space, and 12 acres of open space. The 91.2% occupied property features 86 surface-level parking spaces, which is a rare amenity for street-front retail in this dense urban corridor. Clybourn Center’s tenant mix is anchored by medical and wellness uses, with 38% of the gross leasable area occupied by medical tenants. The post JLL Arranges Sale of Lincoln Park Retail Center to Curbline Properties appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: JLL sold 33K SF unanchored strip center (Clybourn Center) to Curbline Properties.
Property 91.2% occupied; 38% of GLA is medical/wellness tenants.
Adjacent to Foundry Park: $1B+ mixed-use, 3,700 units, 350K SF office, 420K SF retail.
stories.summary: JLL arranged the sale of Clybourn Center, a 33,140-square-foot unanchored strip center in Chicago's Lincoln Park, to Curbline Properties. The property is 91.2% occupied with 38% of space dedicated to medical/wellness tenants. It sits across from Foundry Park, a transformational $1B+ mixed-use redevelopment planned to deliver 3,700 residential units, 350K SF office, 420K SF retail, and 12 acres of open space.
stories.impact: Indirect multifamily demand signal for Lincoln Park/Chicago North Side. Foundry Park's 3,700-unit pipeline, alongside existing density and mixed-use retail/office, signals strong residential demand in this submarket. Clybourn Center's medical/wellness tenant base (38% GLA) may face displacement risk as nearby residential density and foot traffic increase; this could upzone retail rents or force conversions. Monitor Foundry Park delivery timeline and pre-leasing; success will validate rent/absorption assumptions for adjacent residential projects.

stories.title: MCA Realty Acquires 138K-SF Seattle-Area Industrial Portfolio
stories.excerpt: MCA Realty, Inc., a real estate investment company based in California, announced the acquisition of West Valley Distribution Center, a three-building 138,296-square-foot multi-tenant industrial portfolio located in Kent, Washington. The firm acquired the property from Tamarisk West Valley LLC with its MCA Realty Industrial Growth Fund II. MCA now owns three industrial properties encompassing 268,843 square feet in the Seattle MSA. Newmark’s Andrew Briner, Aaron Banks and Kevin Shannon represented both MCA Realty and the seller in the transaction. The firm’s Taylor Hoff and Thad Mallory have been retained to oversee leasing at the property.MCA Realty will make significant exterior and interior improvements. It also plans to reconfigure the suites. “This acquisition reflects our view that the Kent Valley will remain an attractive location for industrial occupants,” said Tyler Mattox, Principal at MCA Realty. “The property’s infill location, durable tenant demand, and upside potential make it a compelling addition to our portfolio.” Hear from the West Coast’s Leading Industrial Real Estate Experts | August 20Gain firsthand insight from the executives, investors, and developers navigating today’s industrial market. From shifting capital flows and debt trends to evolving occupier demand and development strategies, take part in the conversations shaping the future of industrial real estate. Register now: www.connectindustrialwest2026.com The post MCA Realty Acquires 138K-SF Seattle-Area Industrial Portfolio appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: MCA Realty acquired 138K SF industrial portfolio (West Valley Distribution Center), Kent, WA.
Three-building multi-tenant portfolio; MCA now owns 269K SF across Seattle MSA.
MCA plans exterior/interior improvements and suite reconfigurations.
stories.summary: MCA Realty acquired West Valley Distribution Center, a three-building, 138,296-square-foot multi-tenant industrial portfolio in Kent, Washington, using its Industrial Growth Fund II. The firm now owns 268,843 square feet across three properties in the Seattle MSA. MCA plans capital improvements and suite reconfiguration to attract tenants; strong infill location and durable tenant demand cited as rationale.
stories.impact: No direct multifamily impact. Industrial acquisition in Seattle does not signal residential demand, supply, or capital trends. Indirectly: Kent Valley industrial strength suggests warehouse/logistics occupier demand remains robust, possibly indicating continued e-commerce/supply-chain activity; this may support underlying wage/job growth for Seattle metro, but no quantifiable multifamily signal.

stories.title: Rithm Capital Lands $515M Financing for Midtown Office Tower
stories.excerpt: Cushman & Wakefield and Newmark announced that their respective Equity, Debt & Structured Finance teams advised Rithm Capital in securing $515 million in fixed-rate financing for 31 W. 52nd St., a 29-story, 785,000-square-foot office tower in Midtown Manhattan. The Cushman & Wakefield team of Gideon Gil, Zach Kraft and Cecelia Galligan co-brokered the financing alongside Newmark’s Jordan Roeschlaub, Nick Scribani, Adam Spies, Adam Doneger, Tim Polglase, Dan Axelson and Jack Fenton. Led by Wells Fargo, the financing package consists of a $415-million senior mortgage, a $40 million B-Note and a $60-million mezzanine loan. The lending group also includes Bank of America, Barclays, Citi, Goldman Sachs and JPMorgan. The refinancing follows Rithm Capital’s acquisition of the larger Paramount Group office portfolio. “Securing financing of this scale from a consortium of leading lenders demonstrates continued confidence in well-positioned office assets in New York City’s premier business districts,” said Gil. “As capital markets continue to evolve, institutional lenders remain focused on high-quality properties backed by experienced ownership and strong long-term fundamentals.” The post Rithm Capital Lands $515M Financing for Midtown Office Tower appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Rithm Capital secures $515M financing for 31 W. 52nd St., Midtown Manhattan office.
785K SF, 29-story tower; $415M senior, $40M B-Note, $60M mezzanine from consortium.
Wells Fargo, BoA, Barclays, Citi, GS, JPM participating; refinance of Paramount portfolio.
stories.summary: Rithm Capital secured $515M in fixed-rate financing for 31 W. 52nd St., a 785,000-square-foot, 29-story office tower in Midtown Manhattan. The financing structure includes a $415M senior mortgage, $40M B-Note, and $60M mezzanine from a consortium led by Wells Fargo and including major banks. This refinance follows Rithm's acquisition of the broader Paramount Group office portfolio.
stories.impact: Tangential multifamily capital signal. $515M financing for trophy Midtown office at strong terms (consortium of major lenders, fixed-rate, mezzanine availability) suggests institutional capital remains willing to fund prime commercial assets. However, this may crowd out multifamily financing or signal that office repositioning (not conversion) is the preferred strategy; if Paramount diverts capital from residential development, apartment supply in NYC may slow. Monitor whether Rithm/Paramount pursue office-to-residential conversions or stabilize/hold office; conversion would unlock East Midtown residential supply.

stories.title: Auto Parts Distributor Signs $42M Lease in Southern California
stories.excerpt: An auto parts distribution company is expanding in Southern California and has signed a lease to take over a 522,267-square-foot industrial building in the Inland Empire. The lease agreement with landlord Invesco is valued at more than $42 million, according to Daum Commercial Real Estate Services, which announced the deal Thursday and represented the tenant alongside Lara CRE. Daum said the lease represents a significant expansion of the tenant’s Southern California distribution footprint, but did not disclose the company’s name or the length of the lease. The property at 16380 Euclid Avenue is Building 3 at the Chino South Industrial Center in Chino, Calif. It was constructed in 2014 with 32-foot clear heights and dock-high loading bays. Chino’s direct industrial vacancy was at 3.24 percent in the first quarter with limited availability for large distribution space, according to Daum’s research. Daum’s Gus Andros represented the tenant along with Nathan Lara, Andrew Lara, Rudy Lara and Jack Nersesian of Lara CRE. Steve Bellitti, Tom Taylor, Joey Jones and Evelyn Manning of Colliers represented Invesco. Colliers did not immediately return a request for comment. Gregory Cornfield can be reached at gcornfield@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: Auto parts distributor signs $42M lease for 522K SF distribution center, Chino, CA.
Chino direct industrial vacancy 3.24% (Q1 2025); limited large distribution space.
Property: 2014 vintage, 32-ft clear heights, dock-high bays; held by Invesco.
stories.summary: An undisclosed auto parts distributor signed a $42M+ lease for a 522,267-square-foot industrial building in Chino, Calif., representing a significant expansion of the tenant's Southern California distribution footprint. Chino's direct industrial vacancy stands at 3.24% with minimal availability for large distribution space. The property, built in 2014, features 32-foot clear heights and dock-high loading.
stories.impact: Indirect multifamily cost signal for Southern California. Tight Inland Empire industrial vacancy (3.24%) and strong distribution occupancy suggest robust e-commerce/logistics demand, supporting wage growth and inbound worker migration to Inland Empire. This underpins multifamily rent and occupancy fundamentals in the region but does not directly affect supply or capital. However, rising logistics activity may inflate regional construction costs (labor, equipment, materials) if supply chains remain tight; monitor trade/tariff impacts on SoCal construction labor availability and wage pressure.
